name: Tiberinus
description: Tiberinus, also known as Tiberinus Pater, was the Roman god of the Tiber River. He was a significant figure in Roman mythology, representing the vital waterway that sustained Rome and played a crucial role in its founding and development. Tiberinus was believed to possess divine power over the river's flow, its fertility, and its connection to the city's destiny. He was often depicted as a strong, bearded man reclining against an urn, symbolizing the river's source, or holding an oar, representing his control over its currents. Tiberinus was venerated with sacrifices and offerings, particularly during times of flood or drought, to ensure the river's continued benevolence. He is remembered for his role in the story of Romulus and Remus, rescuing them from the flood and delivering them to the care of the she-wolf Lupa. Later, he appeared to Aeneas in a dream, guiding him to the location where Rome would be founded. Tiberinus's presence underscores the Romans' deep connection to their environment and their belief in the divine power of nature.
subtitle: God of the Tiber River

keyMyths.title: Rescue of Romulus and Remus
keyMyths.description: Tiberinus rescued the infant Romulus and Remus from the flood. He ensured their survival by guiding them to the Palatine Hill.

keyMyths.title: Guidance to Aeneas
keyMyths.description: Tiberinus appeared to Aeneas in a dream, advising him to settle in the area where Rome would later be founded. He promised Aeneas safe passage and guidance.

keyMyths.title: Temple and Veneration
keyMyths.description: A temple was dedicated to Tiberinus near the Tiber River. Offerings were made to ensure the river's continued prosperity and prevent floods.

sources.author: Livy
sources.description: A comprehensive history of Rome from its founding.
sources.text: A comprehensive history of Rome from its founding.

sources.author: Virgil
sources.description: An epic poem recounting the journey of Aeneas to Italy.

worshipPractices.name: Votive Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Worshippers would offer votive gifts such as coins, pottery, and small statues into the Tiber River as a sign of respect and to seek Tiberinus' favor. These offerings were often made to ensure safe passage on the river or for bountiful harvests.

worshipPractices.name: Libations
worshipPractices.description: Libations of wine or milk were poured into the Tiber River as a form of sacrifice and tribute to Tiberinus. This practice was meant to appease the god and ensure the river's continued flow and benevolence.

festivals.name: Tiberinalia
festivals.description: The Tiberinalia was an annual festival dedicated to Tiberinus. During this festival, prayers and sacrifices were offered to the god to ensure the river's continued prosperity and to prevent floods. It was a significant event for those who depended on the Tiber for their livelihoods.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Tiber River remains an important symbol of Rome, and Tiberinus is occasionally invoked in modern art and literature as a representation of the city's history and enduring connection to its river. The river's name itself serves as a constant reminder of the deity.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origin of Tiberinus is shrouded in mythological accounts. He is often said to be the son of Janus, the god of beginnings and transitions, reflecting the Tiber's role as a vital starting point for Rome's civilization. Other accounts suggest that he was a king of Alba Longa, who drowned in the Albula river, which was then renamed the Tiber in his honor. This version emphasizes the river's connection to the early Latin kings and the mythical pre-history of Rome. Regardless of his precise parentage, Tiberinus' birth is inextricably linked to the very essence of the river and its importance to the region. He embodies the river's power, its life-giving properties, and its potential for both destruction and sustenance. His mythological origins underscore the sacred status of the Tiber within Roman culture.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One of the most significant myths involving Tiberinus is his role in the story of Romulus and Remus. After being ordered to be drowned by King Amulius, the twins were cast into the Tiber River. Tiberinus, recognizing their divine lineage (being sons of Mars), calmed the waters and ensured their safe passage to the Palatine Hill, where they were discovered by the she-wolf Lupa. This act of divine intervention highlights Tiberinus' protective nature and his crucial role in the founding of Rome. Another story recounts how Tiberinus appeared to Aeneas in a dream, guiding him to the future site of Rome and assuring him of a prosperous destiny. These myths solidify Tiberinus' status as a benevolent and prophetic deity, deeply intertwined with the fate of Rome and its people. His actions shape the course of Roman history, solidifying his importance within the Roman pantheon.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: The worship of Tiberinus was deeply ingrained in the daily lives of Romans, particularly those who lived along the river's banks or depended on it for their livelihoods. Shrines and altars dedicated to Tiberinus were erected near the river, where people would offer prayers, sacrifices, and votive gifts. The Tiberinalia, celebrated annually on August 17th, was a major festival dedicated to the river god. During this festival, Romans would offer sacrifices and libations to Tiberinus, seeking his blessings for a bountiful harvest and protection from floods. The office of *Curator Tiberis* was established to oversee the river's maintenance and to ensure its navigability, further demonstrating the importance of Tiberinus in Roman society. The reverence for Tiberinus reflected the Romans' dependence on the river for transportation, agriculture, and their connection to the wider world. The priests of the cult were responsible for interpreting the river's signs and ensuring its continued favor.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Tiberinus' legacy extends beyond the confines of ancient Roman religion. The Tiber River remains a vital symbol of Rome, and its name serves as a constant reminder of the river god. The image of Tiberinus has appeared in various artistic representations throughout history, often depicted as a strong, bearded man reclining against an urn, symbolizing the river's source. His influence can also be seen in the numerous bridges and structures built across the Tiber, testaments to the Romans' efforts to harness and control the river's power. While formal worship of Tiberinus has ceased, the river continues to hold a significant place in the cultural identity of Rome. Modern artists and writers often invoke Tiberinus to represent the city's rich history and its enduring connection to its river. The river itself stands as a powerful reminder of the deity and his importance in shaping Roman civilization.

powers: Fertility of the land near the river
Granting safe passage on the river
shortDescription: Tiberinus was the Roman god of the Tiber River, a vital waterway for Rome's foundation and growth. He embodied the river's power and connection to the city's destiny.
